300.prompt: Start with this detail: Lists externalize memory. Which idea gives the best comparison?
300.visibleConnection: The question starts from a concrete detail in Why humans make lists: Lists externalize memory. It is really asking which answer handles a similar pressure. Lists as control systems is the best connection because it imposes order on messy reality by sorting tasks, priorities, and attention into a sequence that looks manageable. Both ideas ask alpacas to notice that Lists calm the mind by moving burdens onto paper, but the page can become a mirror of everything still not done.
300.sourceNote: Level 300 retained for later review; 100/200 answer-choice pass did not rewrite this row.

300.prompt: Which idea gives the clearest comparison to Historical list traditions?
300.visibleConnection: The question starts from a concrete detail in Historical list traditions: Inventories and ledgers supported early administration. It is really asking which answer handles a similar pressure. Task methods and prioritization is the best connection because it turns limited time into a sorting problem, deciding which tasks deserve action and which must be sacrificed. Both ideas ask alpacas to notice that Lists are never only practical. They show what a culture wants to preserve, audit, rank, or control.

300.prompt: Which idea turns the same pressure behind The Zeigarnik Effect into a different form?
300.explanation: Correct: Done lists and visible accomplishment.
300.visibleCorrectExplanation: Correct: Done lists and visible accomplishment.
300.visibleConnection: The question starts from a concrete detail in The Zeigarnik Effect: Incomplete tasks may feel mentally louder than completed ones. It is really asking which answer handles a similar pressure. Done lists and visible accomplishment is the best connection because it restores evidence of completed labor that ordinary to-do lists erase the moment a task is crossed off. Both ideas ask alpacas to notice that Unfinished tasks have social and mental power because they keep asking for attention after action has stopped.
300.sourceNote: Level 300 retained for later review; 100/200 answer-choice pass did not rewrite this row.

300.prompt: Which idea gives the clearest comparison for Bucket lists and aspirational lists?
300.visibleConnection: The question starts from a concrete detail in Bucket lists and aspirational lists: Bucket lists are tied to mortality and aspiration. It is really asking which answer handles a similar pressure. Why humans make lists is the best connection because it turns mental overload into an external sequence, then makes the unfinished items easier to see than the completed work. Both ideas ask alpacas to notice that A bucket list can inspire life, but it can also turn desire into a performance of having lived correctly.

300.prompt: Which idea carries the same logic as Lists as control systems?
300.visibleConnection: The question starts from a concrete detail in Lists as control systems: Lists break big goals into smaller units. It is really asking which answer handles a similar pressure. The Zeigarnik Effect is the best connection because it keeps unfinished work mentally active, making incompletion louder than completion in memory. Both ideas ask alpacas to notice that A list looks humble, but it decides sequence, importance, and what disappears from attention.
